expanded_description: Synthesize the major theorems of circle geometry—angles, arcs, and segments—and apply these relationships to master fundamental compass-and-straightedge constructions, specifically for drawing tangents and identifying centers.

core_concept.explanation: Circle geometry is an interconnected system where position determines function. Angles depend on arcs (1:1 for central, 1:2 for inscribed, half-difference for exterior). Segments depend on intersection products (equal inside, whole-external outside). Tangents act as the boundary bridge, always meeting the radius at a strict 90° angle. This section reviews these connections and demonstrates how to physically construct them.

image.alt: Complex diagram showing a central angle, inscribed angle, exterior secant angle, and a tangent line all in one circle with formulas labeled
image.caption: Every theorem relates back to the position of the vertex and the nature of the intercepted arcs

sections.section_title: Theorem Synthesis: Position Determines the Rule

image.alt: Three-panel comparison: Vertex on circle (1/2 arc), Inside (1/2 sum), Outside (1/2 difference)
image.caption: Classifying the vertex location is the single most important step in solving circle problems

sections.introduction: Before moving to constructions, review the three fundamental behaviors of angles based on vertex location. This matrix summarizes Unit 9.

key_concept.definition: The Measure Rule: On Circle = $\frac{1}{2}$ Arc. Inside Circle = $\frac{1}{2}(\text{Arc}_1 + \text{Arc}_2)$. Outside Circle = $\frac{1}{2}(\text{Far Arc} - \text{Near Arc})$. The Tangent Rule: A tangent line is always perpendicular to the radius drawn to the point of tangency.
key_concept.context: These rules are exhaustive. If you can identify where the lines meet, you know which formula to apply immediately.

image.alt: Checklist graphic highlighting the three distinct regions relative to the circle boundary
image.caption: Misidentifying the region is the most common source of calculation error

classifications.value: Multiplication rules based on intersection point
characteristics: Intersection Inside: $a \cdot b = c \cdot d$ (part times part equals part times part)
classifications.behavior: Inside uses individual pieces. Outside always uses the full length from the external point to the far side.

image.alt: Visual comparison showing chord segments inside vs secant lengths outside
image.caption: Inside uses pieces; Outside uses full lengths

sections.section_title: Construction: Tangent at a Point ON the Circle

image.alt: Step-by-step construction showing drawing radius, then using compass to create perpendicular line at the point on circumference
image.caption: A tangent at a point P is simply the line perpendicular to radius OP at point P

sections.introduction: Constructing a tangent requires the property that tangents are perpendicular to radii. This construction utilizes the standard 'construct a perpendicular to a line' method.

key_concept.definition: To construct a tangent at point $P$ on a circle: 1. Draw radius $OP$ and extend it past $P$. 2. Construct a perpendicular line to the radius at point $P$.
key_concept.context: The compass construction involves drawing arcs equidistant from $P$ on the radius line, then arcs intersecting above and below to find the perpendicular path.

image.alt: Close up of compass marks creating a 90 degree angle at the circle's edge
image.caption: Geometric precision relies on the 90 degree relationship

worked_examples.title: Constructing a Tangent at Point P
worked_examples.problem: Construct a line tangent to circle $C$ at point $P$ on the circle.

step_by_step.calculation: Draw line segment connecting Center $C$ to Point $P$. Extend slightly past $P$.
Draw arcs intersecting the radius line on both sides of $P$.
From the two intersection points, swing arcs that cross each other away from the center.
Connect the crossing arcs through point $P$. This is the tangent.

image.alt: Final construction showing the clean tangent line touching the circle only at P
image.caption: The line must touch exactly at P and never enter the circle

sections.section_title: Construction: Tangent from a Point OUTSIDE the Circle

image.alt: Step-by-step construction: Connect external point to center, find midpoint, draw semi-circle, connect intersection points
image.caption: The 'Middle Point' method uses the Inscribed Angle Theorem (angles in a semicircle are 90°)

sections.introduction: This is the most classic compass construction. It relies on a hidden theorem: if you draw a circle using the line from the external point to the center as a diameter, the intersection points will form right angles.

key_concept.definition: To construct tangents from point $X$ outside circle $O$: 1. Connect $X$ to $O$. 2. Find midpoint $M$ of $XO$. 3. Draw a new circle centered at $M$ with radius $MO$. 4. Connect $X$ to the intersection points of the two circles.
key_concept.context: Why it works: The intersection points lie on the 'new' circle where $XO$ is the diameter. Therefore, the angle formed at the intersection is $90^\circ$ (Thales' Theorem). Since the radius is perpendicular to the line, the line is a tangent.

image.alt: Diagram highlighting the 90 degree angle formed by the construction at the tangent point
image.caption: The construction forces a 90 degree angle, which defines a tangent

worked_examples.title: Constructing Two Tangents from Point X
worked_examples.problem: Construct the two tangent lines from external point $X$ to circle $C$.

step_by_step.calculation: Swing arcs from $X$ and $C$ to find the midpoint $M$.
Place compass at $M$, radius $MC$. Draw circle. It should pass through $X$ and $C$.
Mark the two points where the new circle crosses the original circle $C$. Draw lines from $X$ through these points.

image.alt: Final diagram showing the original circle, the auxiliary circle, and two clean tangent lines meeting at X
image.caption: The auxiliary circle is the key tool for finding the points of tangency

sections.section_title: Construction: Finding the Center from Chords

image.alt: Circle with two chords drawn, perpendicular bisectors of chords intersecting at the center point
image.caption: The perpendicular bisector of ANY chord passes through the center

sections.introduction: Sometimes the center isn't marked. You can find it using the property that the center is equidistant from all points on the circle.

key_concept.definition: Draw two non-parallel chords. Construct the perpendicular bisector for each chord. The point where the two bisectors intersect is the center of the circle.
key_concept.context: This is useful for restoring broken circular objects (like pottery) or solving geometry problems where only an arc is given.

image.alt: Zoomed view of two bisecting lines crossing at a specific point labeled Center
image.caption: Two chords are sufficient to lock in the unique center point

image.alt: Flowchart: What is given? Arc/Angle? -> Use Angle Theorems. Segment lengths? -> Use Product Theorems. Tangents? -> Check 90 degrees.
image.caption: Use the given data type to select the correct theorem tool

sections.introduction: Reviewing requires avoiding common errors that combine theorems incorrectly.

process_flow.description: Is the problem about Angles or Segments? Is the vertex Inside, On, or Outside?
If Outside: Are you using the WHOLE secant length? If Inside: Are you using the PART lengths?
If a tangent is involved, did you account for the 90-degree angle with the radius? Is it a Right Triangle problem?

categories.value: Errors to avoid on the unit test
characteristics: Confusing the 'Whole' length with the 'Chord' length in external segment problems
Forgetting to double the inscribed angle to find the arc measure
Assuming a line is tangent without a 90-degree marker
categories.note: In constructions, a sketch is not enough. Arc marks must be visible to prove the construction was done with a compass.

image.alt: Diagram showing the composition of the whole secant length
image.caption: Visualizing the segments prevents algebraic errors

summary: This unit culminates in the synthesis of angle and segment relationships and their practical application. Key theorems include: 1) The Angle Rule (Central=Arc, Inscribed=1/2 Arc, Interior=1/2 Sum, Exterior=1/2 Difference). 2) The Segment Rule (Inside: product of parts equal; Outside: whole * external equal). 3) The Tangent Rule (Tangent $\perp$ Radius). Constructions utilize these theorems: The tangent at a point is constructed by creating a perpendicular to the radius. Tangents from an external point are constructed by creating a semicircle on the segment connecting the external point to the center, leveraging Thales' Theorem to force a 90-degree angle. Finally, the center of any circle can be found by intersecting the perpendicular bisectors of any two chords. Mastering these concepts allows for both algebraic solving and precise geometric drawing.
